stem: Which action is performed by a client when establishing communication with a server via the use of UDP at the transport layer?
choices: The client sets the window size for the session.
The client sends an ISN to the server to start the 3-way handshake.
The client randomly selects a source port number.
The client sends a synchronization segment to begin the session.
explain_short: UDP is connectionless — there is no handshake or session setup. The client just chooses a random ephemeral source port and sends datagrams to the server's well-known port.

explain_lecture.quote: The UDP client process dynamically selects a port number from the range of port numbers and uses this as the source port for the conversation.

stem: Which transport layer feature is used to guarantee session establishment?
choices: UDP ACK flag
TCP 3-way handshake
UDP sequence number
TCP port number
explain_short: TCP's three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) negotiates and synchronizes the session before any data is sent. UDP has no session.

explain_lecture.quote: Step 1: The initiating client requests a client-to-server communication session with the server. Step 2: The server acknowledges the client-to-server communication session and requests a server-to-client communication session. Step 3: The initiating client acknowledges the server-to-client communication session.

stem: What is the complete range of TCP and UDP well-known ports?
explain_short: IANA defines well-known ports as 0–1023. Registered ports are 1024–49151; dynamic/private ports are 49152–65535.

stem: What is a socket?
choices: the combination of the source and destination IP address and source and destination Ethernet address
the combination of a source IP address and port number or a destination IP address and port number
the combination of the source and destination sequence and acknowledgment numbers
the combination of the source and destination sequence numbers and port numbers
explain_short: A socket = IP address + port. The combination uniquely identifies one endpoint of a conversation.

explain_lecture.quote: The combination of the source IP address and source port number, or the destination IP address and destination port number is known as a socket.

stem: A PC is downloading a large file from a server. The TCP window is 1000 bytes. The server is sending the file using 100-byte segments. How many segments will the server send before it requires an acknowledgment from the PC?
choices: 1 segment
10 segments
100 segments
1000 segments
explain_short: TCP window = max bytes the sender can transmit before needing an ACK. 1000 ÷ 100 = 10 segments fit in the window, so the server sends 10 segments and then waits for an acknowledgement.

stem: Which factor determines TCP window size?
choices: the amount of data to be transmitted
the number of services included in the TCP segment
the amount of data the destination can process at one time
the amount of data the source is capable of sending at one time
explain_short: TCP window size is the amount of data the destination can receive and process before sending an acknowledgement — set by the receiver based on its buffer/processing capacity.

stem: What does a client do when it has UDP datagrams to send?
choices: It just sends the datagrams.
It queries the server to see if it is ready to receive data.
It sends a simplified three-way handshake to the server.
It sends to the server a segment with the SYN flag set to synchronize the conversation.
explain_short: UDP is connectionless. The client just builds the datagram and sends it — no handshake, no flow control, no acknowledgment.

explain_lecture.quote: UDP does not track sequence numbers the way TCP does. UDP has no way to reorder the datagrams into their transmission order. UDP simply reassembles the data in the order that it was received and forwards it to the application.

stem: Which three fields are used in a UDP segment header? (Choose three.)
choices: Window Size
Length
Source Port
Acknowledgment Number
Checksum
Sequence Number
explain_short: The UDP header has only 4 fields (8 bytes total): source port, destination port, length, and checksum.

explain_lecture.quote: The UDP header is far simpler than the TCP header because it only has four fields and requires 8 bytes (i.e. 64 bits).

stem: What are two roles of the transport layer in data communication on a network? (Choose two.)
choices: identifying the proper application for each communication stream
tracking the individual communication between applications on the source and destination hosts
providing frame delimiting to identify bits making up a frame
performing a cyclic redundancy check on the frame for errors
providing the interface between applications and the underlying network over which messages are transmitted
explain_short: The transport layer tracks individual conversations between applications and segments/reassembles application data into transport-layer PDUs.

explain_lecture.slide: Transportation of Data — Transport Layer Responsibilities
explain_lecture.quote: The transport layer has the following responsibilities: Tracking individual conversations; Segmenting data and reassembling segments; Adds header information; Identify, separate, and manage multiple conversations.

stem: What information is used by TCP to reassemble and reorder received segments?
choices: port numbers
sequence numbers
acknowledgment numbers
fragment numbers
explain_short: TCP uses sequence numbers in the segment header to put segments back into the correct order at the receiver.

explain_lecture.slide: Reliability and Flow Control — TCP Reliability- Guaranteed and Ordered Delivery
explain_lecture.quote: All the data must be received and the data in these segments must be reassembled into the original order. Sequence numbers are assigned in the header of each packet to achieve this goal.

stem: What important information is added to the TCP/IP transport layer header to ensure communication and connectivity with a remote network device?
choices: timing and synchronization
destination and source port numbers
destination and source physical addresses
destination and source logical network addresses
explain_short: Port numbers in the transport-layer header identify the source and destination applications, so the receiver knows which process should get the data.

explain_lecture.quote: The source port number is associated with the originating application on the local host whereas the destination port number is associated with the destination application on the remote host.

stem: Which two characteristics are associated with UDP sessions? (Choose two.)
choices: Destination devices receive traffic with minimal delay.
Transmitted data segments are tracked.
Destination devices reassemble messages and pass them to an application.
Received data is unacknowledged.
Unacknowledged data packets are retransmitted.
explain_short: UDP is connectionless and best-effort: no tracking, no acknowledgements, minimal delay. Lost segments are not retransmitted by UDP itself.

explain_lecture.slide: Transportation of Data — User Datagram Protocol (UDP)
explain_lecture.quote: UDP provides the basic functions for delivering datagrams between the appropriate applications, with very little overhead and data checking. UDP is a connectionless protocol. UDP is known as a best-effort delivery protocol because there is no acknowledgment that the data is received at the destination.

stem: Which flag in the TCP header is used in response to a received FIN in order to terminate connectivity between two network devices?
choices: FIN
ACK
SYN
RST
explain_short: The ACK flag is set in response to a received FIN to acknowledge the half-close. Termination uses two FIN/ACK pairs (one each direction).

explain_lecture.quote: Step 1: When the client has no more data to send in the stream, it sends a segment with the FIN flag set. Step 2: The server sends an ACK to acknowledge the receipt of the FIN to terminate the session from client to server.

stem: Which protocol or service uses UDP for a client-to-server communication and TCP for server-to-server communication?
choices: HTTP
FTP
DNS
SMTP
explain_short: DNS uses UDP port 53 for typical client lookups (small, fast) and TCP port 53 for zone transfers and large responses.

stem: What is a characteristic of UDP?
choices: UDP datagrams take the same path and arrive in the correct order at the destination.​
Applications that use UDP are always considered unreliable.​
UDP reassembles the received datagrams in the order they were received.
UDP only passes data to the network when the destination is ready to receive the data.
explain_short: UDP is connectionless, has no acknowledgements and no reordering — it simply reassembles data in the order it arrives and hands it to the application.

explain_lecture.quote: UDP has no way to reorder the datagrams into their transmission order. UDP simply reassembles the data in the order that it was received and forwards it to the application.

stem: What kind of port must be requested from IANA in order to be used with a specific application?
choices: registered port
private port
dynamic port
source port
explain_short: Registered ports (1024–49151) are assigned by IANA to a requesting entity for use with a specific process or application.

stem: Which three application layer protocols use TCP? (Choose three.)
choices: SMTP
FTP
SNMP
HTTP
TFTP
DHCP
explain_short: Applications that need reliable, in-order delivery use TCP. Common examples: HTTP/HTTPS, SMTP, FTP, Telnet, POP3.

stem: Which three statements characterize UDP? (Choose three.)
choices: UDP provides basic connectionless transport layer functions.
UDP provides connection-oriented, fast transport of data at Layer 3.
UDP relies on application layer protocols for error detection.
UDP is a low overhead protocol that does not provide sequencing or flow control mechanisms.
UDP relies on IP for error detection and recovery.
UDP provides sophisticated flow control mechanisms.
explain_short: UDP is connectionless, lightweight, and stateless — no handshake, no acknowledgements, low overhead, used for time-sensitive traffic.

explain_lecture.quote: UDP features include the following: Data is reconstructed in the order that it is received. Any segments that are lost are not resent. There is no session establishment. The sending is not informed about resource availability.

stem: Which two fields are included in the TCP header but not in the UDP header? (Choose two.)
choices: window
checksum
source port
destination port
sequence number
explain_short: TCP carries Sequence Number and Window fields (for ordering and flow control) which UDP does not.

stem: Which field in the TCP header indicates the status of the three-way handshake process?
choices: window
reserved
checksum
control bits
explain_short: The TCP Control Bits (flags) field carries SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG — these drive the handshake and termination state machine.

explain_lecture.slide: TCP Communication Process — TCP Three-Way Handshake Analysis (Cont.)
explain_lecture.quote: The six control bit flags are as follows: URG - Urgent pointer field significant; ACK - Acknowledgment flag used in connection establishment and session termination; PSH - Push function; RST - Reset the connection when an error or timeout occurs; SYN - Synchronize sequence numbers used in connection establishment; FIN - No more data from sender and used in session termination.

stem: Why does HTTP use TCP as the transport layer protocol?
choices: to ensure the fastest possible download speed
because HTTP is a best-effort protocol
because transmission errors can be tolerated easily
because HTTP requires reliable delivery
explain_short: HTTP delivers web pages where every byte matters and order matters; it relies on TCP to provide reliable, in-order, flow-controlled delivery.

explain_lecture.slide: Transportation of Data — The Right Transport Layer Protocol for the Right Application
explain_lecture.quote: If it is important that all the data arrives and that it can be processed in its proper sequence, TCP is used as the transport protocol.

stem: Which two types of applications are best suited for UDP? (Choose two.)
choices: applications that need data flow control
applications that require reliable delivery
applications that handle reliability themselves
applications that need the reordering of segments
applications that can tolerate some data loss, but require little or no delay
explain_short: UDP suits apps that tolerate some loss but need low delay (live video/voice) and apps that handle their own reliability (SNMP, TFTP, DNS queries).

explain_lecture.slide: UDP Overview — Applications that use UDP
explain_lecture.quote: Live video and multimedia applications - These applications can tolerate some data loss but require little or no delay. Examples include VoIP and live streaming video. ... Applications that handle reliability themselves - Unidirectional communications where flow control, error detection, acknowledgments, and error recovery is not required, or can be handled by the application. Examples include SNMP and TFTP.

stem: How are port numbers used in the TCP/IP encapsulation process?
choices: Source port numbers and destination port numbers are not necessary when UDP is the transport layer protocol being used for the communication.
Source port and destination port numbers are randomly generated.
If multiple conversations occur that are using the same service, the source port number is used to track the separate conversations.
Destination port numbers are assigned automatically and cannot be changed.
explain_short: Both TCP and UDP encapsulate the segment with a source port (originating app on local host) and a destination port (target app on remote host).

explain_lecture.quote: TCP and UDP transport layer protocols use port numbers to manage multiple, simultaneous conversations.

stem: In what two situations would UDP be better than TCP as the preferred transport protocol? (Choose two.)
choices: when applications need to guarantee that a packet arrives intact, in sequence, and unduplicated
when a faster delivery mechanism is needed
when delivery overhead is not an issue
when applications do not need to guarantee delivery of the data
when destination port numbers are dynamic
explain_short: UDP is preferred for live multimedia (low latency tolerated) and for simple request-reply transactions where retransmission can be done by the application quickly.

explain_lecture.slide: Transportation of Data — The Right Transport Layer Protocol for the Right Application
explain_lecture.quote: UDP is also used by request-and-reply applications where the data is minimal, and retransmission can be done quickly.

stem: What are three responsibilities of the transport layer? (Choose three.)
choices: meeting the reliability requirements of applications, if any
multiplexing multiple communication streams from many users or applications on the same network
identifying the applications and services on the client and server that should handle transmitted data
directing packets towards the destination network
formatting data into a compatible form for receipt by the destination devices
conducting error detection of the contents in frames
explain_short: Transport layer responsibilities include tracking individual conversations, segmenting data and reassembling segments, and identifying applications via port numbers.

explain_lecture.slide: Transportation of Data — Transport Layer Responsibilities
explain_lecture.quote: The transport layer has the following responsibilities: Tracking individual conversations; Segmenting data and reassembling segments; Adds header information; Identify, separate, and manage multiple conversations; Uses segmentation and multiplexing to enable different communication conversations to be interleaved on the same network.

stem: Which scenario describes a function provided by the transport layer?
choices: A student is using a classroom VoIP phone to call home. The unique identifier burned into the phone is a transport layer address used to contact another network device on the same network.
A student is playing a short web-based movie with sound. The movie and sound are encoded within the transport layer header.
A student has two web browser windows open in order to access two web sites. The transport layer ensures the correct web page is delivered to the correct browser window.

A corporate worker is accessing a web server located on a corporate network. The transport layer formats the screen so the web page appears properly no matter what device is being used to view the web site.
explain_short: A core transport-layer function is identifying the source/destination application via port numbers so multiple conversations can run over a single IP.

explain_lecture.quote: TCP and UDP transport layer protocols use port numbers to manage multiple, simultaneous conversations. The source port number is associated with the originating application on the local host whereas the destination port number is associated with the destination application on the remote host.

stem: A PC that is communicating with a web server has a TCP window size of 6,000 bytes when sending data and a packet size of 1,500 bytes. Which byte of information will the web server acknowledge after it has received two packets of data from the PC?
explain_short: Window = 6000, packet = 1500. After 2 packets received (3000 bytes, sequence 1..3000), the next byte expected is 3001 — that is what the server acknowledges.

stem: A PC that is communicating with a web server has a TCP window size of 6,000 bytes when sending data and a packet size of 1,500 bytes. Which byte of information will the web server acknowledge after it has received three packets of data from the PC?
explain_short: After 3 packets (4500 bytes), the next expected byte = 4501. TCP ACKs the next byte expected, not the last byte received.

stem: A PC that is communicating with a web server has a TCP window size of 6,000 bytes when sending data and a packet size of 1,500 bytes. Which byte of information will the web server acknowledge after it has received four packets of data from the PC?
explain_short: After 4 packets (6000 bytes — the full window), the next byte expected = 6001.
